title: Seismologist

infobox.value: ([/saɪzˈmɒlədʒi, saɪs-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") σεισμός (*seismós*), meaning '[earthquake](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake")', and -λογία (*-logía*), meaning 'study of') is the scientific study of [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") (or generally, [quakes](/wiki/Quake_(natural_phenomenon) "Quake (natural phenomenon)")) and the generation and propagation of [elastic waves](/wiki/Linear_elasticity#Elastic_wave "Linear elasticity") through [planetary bodies](/wiki/Planetary_body "Planetary body"). It also includes studies of the [environmental effects of earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake_environmental_effects "Earthquake environmental effects") such as [tsunamis](/wiki/Tsunamis "Tsunamis"); other [seismic sources](/wiki/Seismic_source "Seismic source") such as volcanoes, [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics"), glaciers, [rivers](/wiki/Fluvial_seismology "Fluvial seismology"), oceanic [microseisms](/wiki/Microseism "Microseism"), and the atmosphere; and [artificial processes](/wiki/Induced_seismicity "Induced seismicity") such as explosions.
is a scientist who works in basic or applied seismology.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/2004_Indonesia_Tsunami_Complete.gif/250px-2004_Indonesia_Tsunami_Complete.gif)](/wiki/File:2004_Indonesia_Tsunami_Complete.gif)

Animation of [tsunami](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") triggered by the [2004 Indian Ocean earthquake](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami")

**Seismology** ([/saɪzˈmɒlədʒi, saɪs-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") σεισμός (*seismós*), meaning '[earthquake](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake")', and -λογία (*-logía*), meaning 'study of') is the scientific study of [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") (or generally, [quakes](/wiki/Quake_(natural_phenomenon) "Quake (natural phenomenon)")) and the generation and propagation of [elastic waves](/wiki/Linear_elasticity#Elastic_wave "Linear elasticity") through [planetary bodies](/wiki/Planetary_body "Planetary body"). It also includes studies of the [environmental effects of earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake_environmental_effects "Earthquake environmental effects") such as [tsunamis](/wiki/Tsunamis "Tsunamis"); other [seismic sources](/wiki/Seismic_source "Seismic source") such as volcanoes, [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics"), glaciers, [rivers](/wiki/Fluvial_seismology "Fluvial seismology"), oceanic [microseisms](/wiki/Microseism "Microseism"), and the atmosphere; and [artificial processes](/wiki/Induced_seismicity "Induced seismicity") such as explosions.

[Paleoseismology](/wiki/Paleoseismology "Paleoseismology") is a related field that uses [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology") to infer information regarding past earthquakes. A recording of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s motion as a function of time, created by a [seismograph](/wiki/Seismograph "Seismograph") is called a [seismogram](/wiki/Seismogram "Seismogram"). A **seismologist** is a scientist who works in basic or applied seismology.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Ancient and classical eras

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ancient and classical eras")]

Scholarly interest in earthquakes can be traced back to antiquity. Early speculations on the natural causes of earthquakes were included in the writings of [Thales](/wiki/Thales "Thales") of Miletus (c. 585 BCE), [Anaximenes of Miletus](/wiki/Anaximenes_of_Miletus "Anaximenes of Miletus") (c. 550 BCE), [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") (c. 340 BCE), and [Zhang Heng](/wiki/Zhang_Heng "Zhang Heng") (132 CE).

In 132 CE, Zhang Heng of China's [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") designed the first known [seismoscope](/wiki/Seismoscope "Seismoscope").[[1]](#cite_note-needham-1)[[2]](#cite_note-DeweyByerly-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Beginnings of modern science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Beginnings of modern science")]

In the 17th century, [Athanasius Kircher](/wiki/Athanasius_Kircher "Athanasius Kircher") argued that earthquakes were caused by the movement of fire within a system of channels inside the Earth. [Martin Lister](/wiki/Martin_Lister "Martin Lister") (1638–1712) and [Nicolas Lemery](/wiki/Nicolas_Lemery "Nicolas Lemery") (1645–1715) proposed that earthquakes were caused by chemical explosions within the Earth.[[4]](#cite_note-Lisbon-4)

The [Lisbon earthquake of 1755](/wiki/1755_Lisbon_earthquake "1755 Lisbon earthquake"), coinciding with the general flowering of science in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), set in motion intensified scientific attempts to understand the behaviour and causation of earthquakes. The earliest responses include work by [John Bevis](/wiki/John_Bevis "John Bevis") (1757) and [John Michell](/wiki/John_Michell "John Michell") (1761). Michell determined that earthquakes originate within the Earth and were waves of movement caused by "shifting masses of rock miles below the surface".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In response to a series of earthquakes near [Comrie](/wiki/Comrie,_Perth_and_Kinross "Comrie, Perth and Kinross") in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") in 1839, a committee was formed in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") in order to produce better detection methods for earthquakes. The outcome of this was the production of one of the first modern [seismometers](/wiki/Seismometer "Seismometer") by [James David Forbes](/wiki/James_David_Forbes "James David Forbes"), first presented in a report by [David Milne-Home](/wiki/David_Milne-Home "David Milne-Home") in 1842.[[6]](#cite_note-JamesForbes-6) This seismometer was an inverted pendulum, which recorded the measurements of seismic activity through the use of a pencil placed on paper above the pendulum. The designs provided did not prove effective, according to Milne's reports.[[6]](#cite_note-JamesForbes-6)

From 1857, [Robert Mallet](/wiki/Robert_Mallet "Robert Mallet") laid the foundation of modern instrumental seismology and carried out seismological experiments using explosives. He is also responsible for coining the word "seismology."[[7]](#cite_note-7) He is widely considered to be the "Father of Seismology".

In 1889 [Ernst von Rebeur-Paschwitz](/wiki/Ernst_von_Rebeur-Paschwitz "Ernst von Rebeur-Paschwitz") recorded the first teleseismic earthquake signal (an earthquake in Japan recorded at Pottsdam Germany).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 1894 [Fusakichi Omori](/wiki/Fusakichi_Omori "Fusakichi Omori") demonstrated that the frequency of earthquake aftershocks decays following a mainshock, based on his analysis of the [1889 Kumamoto](/wiki/1889_Kumamoto_earthquake "1889 Kumamoto earthquake"), [1891 Mino–Owari](/wiki/1891_Mino%E2%80%93Owari_earthquake "1891 Mino–Owari earthquake") and the 1893 Kagoshima earthquakes[[9]](#cite_note-9).

In 1897, [Emil Wiechert](/wiki/Emil_Wiechert "Emil Wiechert")'s theoretical calculations led him to conclude that the [Earth's interior](/wiki/Structure_of_the_Earth "Structure of the Earth") consists of a mantle of silicates, surrounding a core of iron.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 1906 [Richard Dixon Oldham](/wiki/Richard_Dixon_Oldham "Richard Dixon Oldham") identified the separate arrival of [P waves](/wiki/P_wave "P wave"), S waves and surface waves on seismograms and found the first clear evidence that the Earth has a central core.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1909, [Andrija Mohorovičić](/wiki/Andrija_Mohorovi%C4%8Di%C4%87 "Andrija Mohorovičić"), one of the founders of modern seismology,[[12]](#cite_note-Penn_state-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia.com-13)[[14]](#cite_note-ssn-14) discovered and defined the [Mohorovičić discontinuity](/wiki/Mohorovi%C4%8Di%C4%87_discontinuity "Mohorovičić discontinuity").[[15]](#cite_note-McLeish-15) Usually referred to as the "Moho discontinuity" or the "[Moho](/wiki/Mohorovi%C4%8Di%C4%87_discontinuity "Mohorovičić discontinuity")," it is the boundary between the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s [crust](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") and the [mantle](/wiki/Mantle_(geology) "Mantle (geology)"). It is defined by the distinct change in velocity of seismological waves as they pass through changing densities of rock.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In 1910, after studying the April [1906 San Francisco earthquake](/wiki/1906_San_Francisco_earthquake "1906 San Francisco earthquake"), [Harry Fielding Reid](/wiki/Harry_Fielding_Reid "Harry Fielding Reid") put forward the "[elastic rebound theory](/wiki/Elastic-rebound_theory "Elastic-rebound theory")" which remains the foundation for modern tectonic studies. The development of this theory depended on the considerable progress of earlier independent streams of work on the behavior of elastic materials and in mathematics.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

An early scientific study of [aftershocks](/wiki/Aftershock "Aftershock") from a destructive earthquake came after the January [1920 Xalapa earthquake](/wiki/1920_Xalapa_earthquake "1920 Xalapa earthquake"). An 80 kg (180 lb) Wiechert seismograph was brought to the Mexican city of Xalapa by rail after the earthquake. The instrument was deployed to record its aftershocks. Data from the seismograph would eventually determine that the mainshock was produced along a shallow crustal fault.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In 1926, [Harold Jeffreys](/wiki/Harold_Jeffreys "Harold Jeffreys") was the first to claim, based on his study of earthquake waves, that below the mantle, the core of the Earth is liquid.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 1937, [Inge Lehmann](/wiki/Inge_Lehmann "Inge Lehmann") determined that within Earth's liquid [outer core](/wiki/Outer_core "Outer core") there is a solid [inner core](/wiki/Inner_core "Inner core").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 1950, [Michael S. Longuet-Higgins](/wiki/Michael_S._Longuet-Higgins "Michael S. Longuet-Higgins") elucidated the ocean processes responsible for the global background seismic [microseism](/wiki/Microseism "Microseism").[[21]](#cite_note-LonguetHiggins-21)

By the 1960s, Earth science had developed to the point where a comprehensive theory of the causation of seismic events and geodetic motions had come together in the now well-established theory of [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Types of seismic wave

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Types of seismic wave")]

Main article: [Seismic wave](/wiki/Seismic_wave "Seismic wave")

[![Three lines with frequent vertical excursions.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Seismogram.gif/330px-Seismogram.gif)](/wiki/File:Seismogram.gif)

Seismogram records showing the three components of [ground motion](/wiki/Ground_motion "Ground motion"). The red line marks the first arrival of P waves; the green line, the later arrival of S waves.

Seismic waves are [elastic waves](/wiki/Elastic_waves "Elastic waves") that propagate in solid or fluid materials. They can be divided into *body waves* that travel through the interior of the materials; *surface waves* that travel along surfaces or interfaces between materials; and *normal modes*, a form of standing wave.

### Body waves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Body waves")]

There are two types of body waves, pressure waves or primary waves (P waves) and [shear](/wiki/Shearing_(physics) "Shearing (physics)") or secondary waves ([S waves](/wiki/S_wave "S wave")). P waves are [longitudinal waves](/wiki/Longitudinal_wave "Longitudinal wave") associated with [compression](/wiki/Compression_(geology) "Compression (geology)") and [expansion](/wiki/Rarefaction "Rarefaction"), and involve particle motion parallel to the direction of wave propagation. P waves are always the first waves to appear on a seismogram as they are the waves that travel fastest through solids. [S waves](/wiki/S_waves "S waves") are [transverse waves](/wiki/Transverse_wave "Transverse wave") associated with shear, and involve particle motion perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. S waves travel more slowly than P waves so they appear later than P waves on a seismogram. Because of their low shear strength, fluids cannot support transverse elastic waves, so S waves travel only in solids.[[23]](#cite_note-Gubbins1990-23)

### Surface waves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Surface waves")]

Surface waves are the result of P and S waves interacting with the surface of the Earth. These waves are [dispersive](/wiki/Dispersion_(optics) "Dispersion (optics)"), meaning that different frequencies have different velocities. The two main surface wave types are [Rayleigh waves](/wiki/Rayleigh_wave "Rayleigh wave"), which have both compressional and shear motions, and [Love waves](/wiki/Love_wave "Love wave"), which are purely shear. Rayleigh waves result from the interaction of P waves and vertically polarized S waves with the surface and can exist in any solid medium. Love waves are formed by horizontally polarized S waves interacting with the surface, and can only exist if there is a change in the elastic properties with depth in a solid medium, which is always the case in seismological applications. Surface waves travel more slowly than P waves and S waves because they are the result of these waves traveling along indirect paths to interact with Earth's surface. Because they travel along the surface of the Earth, their energy decays less rapidly than body waves (1/distance2 vs. 1/distance3), and thus the shaking caused by surface waves is generally stronger than that of body waves, and the primary surface waves are often thus the largest signals on earthquake [seismograms](/wiki/Seismogram "Seismogram"). Surface waves are strongly excited when their source is close to the surface, as in a shallow earthquake or a near-surface explosion, and are much weaker for deep earthquake sources.[[23]](#cite_note-Gubbins1990-23)

### Normal modes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Normal modes")]

See also: [Free oscillations of the Earth](/wiki/Seismic_wave#Free_oscillations_of_the_Earth "Seismic wave")

Both body and surface waves are traveling waves; however, large earthquakes can also make the entire Earth "ring" like a resonant bell. This ringing is a mixture of [normal modes](/wiki/Normal_modes "Normal modes") with discrete frequencies and periods of approximately an hour or shorter. Normal-mode motion caused by a very large earthquake can be observed for up to a month after the event.[[23]](#cite_note-Gubbins1990-23) The first observations of normal modes were made in the 1960s as the advent of higher-fidelity instruments coincided with two of the largest earthquakes of the 20th century, the [1960 Valdivia earthquake](/wiki/1960_Valdivia_earthquake "1960 Valdivia earthquake") and the [1964 Alaska earthquake](/wiki/1964_Alaska_earthquake "1964 Alaska earthquake"). Since then, the normal modes of the Earth have given us some of the strongest constraints on the deep structure of the Earth.

## Earthquakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Earthquakes")]

Main articles: [Earthquake](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") and [Lists of earthquakes](/wiki/Lists_of_earthquakes "Lists of earthquakes")

One of the first attempts at the scientific study of earthquakes followed the [1755 Lisbon earthquake](/wiki/1755_Lisbon_earthquake "1755 Lisbon earthquake"). Other earthquakes that spurred major advancements in the science of seismology include the [1857 Basilicata earthquake](/wiki/1857_Basilicata_earthquake "1857 Basilicata earthquake"), the [1906 San Francisco earthquake](/wiki/1906_San_Francisco_earthquake "1906 San Francisco earthquake"), the [1964 Alaska earthquake](/wiki/1964_Alaska_earthquake "1964 Alaska earthquake"), the 2004 [Sumatra-Andaman earthquake](/wiki/Sumatra-Andaman_earthquake "Sumatra-Andaman earthquake"), and the 2011 [Great East Japan earthquake](/wiki/Tohoku_earthquake "Tohoku earthquake").

## Controlled seismic sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Controlled seismic sources")]

See also: [Reflection seismology](/wiki/Reflection_seismology "Reflection seismology")

Seismic waves produced by [explosions](/wiki/Explosion "Explosion") or vibrating controlled sources are one of the primary methods of [underground exploration in geophysics](/wiki/Exploration_geophysics "Exploration geophysics") (in addition to many different [electromagnetic](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") methods such as [induced polarization](/wiki/Induced_polarization "Induced polarization") and [magnetotellurics](/wiki/Magnetotellurics "Magnetotellurics")). Controlled-source seismology has been used to map [salt domes](/wiki/Salt_dome "Salt dome"), anticlines and other geologic traps in [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum")-bearing [rocks](/wiki/Rock_(geology) "Rock (geology)"), [faults](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)"), rock types, and long-buried giant [meteor](/wiki/Meteor "Meteor") [craters](/wiki/Impact_crater "Impact crater"). For example, the [Chicxulub Crater](/wiki/Chicxulub_Crater "Chicxulub Crater"), which was caused by an impact that has been [implicated in the extinction](/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event "Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event") of the [dinosaurs](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur"), was localized to Central America by analyzing ejecta in the [Cretaceous–Paleogene boundary](/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_boundary "Cretaceous–Paleogene boundary"), and then physically proven to exist using seismic maps from [oil exploration](/wiki/Oil_exploration "Oil exploration").[[24]](#cite_note-Schulte2010-24)

## Detection of seismic waves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Detection of seismic waves")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Seismometer-iceland.JPG/250px-Seismometer-iceland.JPG)](/wiki/File:Seismometer-iceland.JPG)

Installation for a temporary seismic station, north Iceland highland.

[Seismometers](/wiki/Seismometer "Seismometer") are sensors that detect and record the motion of the Earth arising from elastic waves. Seismometers may be deployed at the Earth's surface, in shallow vaults, in boreholes, or [underwater](/wiki/Ocean-bottom_seismometer "Ocean-bottom seismometer"). A complete instrument package that records seismic signals is called a [seismograph](/wiki/Seismograph "Seismograph"). Networks of seismographs continuously record ground motions around the world to facilitate the monitoring and analysis of global earthquakes and other sources of seismic activity. Rapid location of earthquakes makes [tsunami](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") warnings possible because seismic waves travel considerably faster than tsunami waves.

Seismometers also record signals from non-earthquake sources ranging from explosions (nuclear and chemical), to local noise from wind[[25]](#cite_note-25) or anthropogenic activities, to incessant signals generated at the ocean floor and coasts induced by ocean waves (the global [microseism](/wiki/Microseism "Microseism")), to [cryospheric](/wiki/Cryosphere "Cryosphere") events associated with large [icebergs](/wiki/Iceberg "Iceberg") and glaciers. Above-ocean meteor strikes with energies as high as 4.2 × 1013 [J](/wiki/Joule "Joule") (equivalent to that released by an explosion of ten kilotons of TNT) have been recorded by seismographs, as have a number of industrial accidents and terrorist bombs and events (a field of study referred to as [forensic seismology](/wiki/Forensic_seismology "Forensic seismology")). A major long-term motivation for the global seismographic monitoring has been for the detection and study of [nuclear testing](/wiki/Nuclear_testing "Nuclear testing").

## Mapping Earth's interior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Mapping Earth's interior")]

Main article: [Earth's interior](/wiki/Earth%27s_interior "Earth's interior")

[![Diagram with concentric shells and curved paths](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Earthquake_wave_paths.svg/330px-Earthquake_wave_paths.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Earthquake_wave_paths.svg)

Seismic velocities and boundaries in the interior of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") sampled by seismic waves

Because seismic waves commonly propagate efficiently as they interact with the internal structure of the Earth, they provide high-resolution noninvasive methods for studying the planet's interior. One of the earliest important discoveries (suggested by [Richard Dixon Oldham](/wiki/Richard_Dixon_Oldham "Richard Dixon Oldham") in 1906 and definitively shown by Harold Jeffreys in 1926) was that the [outer core](/wiki/Outer_core "Outer core") of the earth is liquid. Since S waves do not pass through liquids, the liquid core causes a "shadow" on the side of the planet opposite the earthquake where no direct S waves are observed. In addition, P waves travel much slower through the outer core than the mantle.

Processing readings from many seismometers using [seismic tomography](/wiki/Seismic_tomography "Seismic tomography"), seismologists have mapped the mantle of the earth to a resolution of several hundred kilometers. This has enabled scientists to identify [convection cells](/wiki/Convection_cell "Convection cell") and other large-scale features such as the [large low-shear-velocity provinces](/wiki/Large_low-shear-velocity_provinces "Large low-shear-velocity provinces") near the [core–mantle boundary](/wiki/Core%E2%80%93mantle_boundary "Core–mantle boundary").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Seismology and society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Seismology and society")]

### Earthquake prediction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Earthquake prediction")]

Main article: [Earthquake prediction](/wiki/Earthquake_prediction "Earthquake prediction")

Public controversy over earthquake prediction erupted after Italian authorities [indicted](/wiki/Indicted "Indicted") six seismologists and one government official for [manslaughter](/wiki/Manslaughter "Manslaughter") in connection with [a magnitude 6.3 earthquake in L'Aquila, Italy on April 5, 2009](/wiki/2009_L%27Aquila_earthquake "2009 L'Aquila earthquake").[[27]](#cite_note-Hall_2011-27) A report in [Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)") stated that the indictment was widely seen in Italy and abroad as being for failing to predict the earthquake and drew condemnation from the [American Association for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/American_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "American Association for the Advancement of Science") and the [American Geophysical Union](/wiki/American_Geophysical_Union "American Geophysical Union").[[27]](#cite_note-Hall_2011-27) However, the magazine also indicated that the population of Aquila do not consider the failure to predict the earthquake to be the reason for the indictment, but rather the alleged failure of the scientists to evaluate and communicate risk.[[27]](#cite_note-Hall_2011-27) The indictment claims that, at a special meeting in [L'Aquila](/wiki/L%27Aquila "L'Aquila") the week before the earthquake occurred, scientists and officials were more interested in pacifying the population than providing adequate information about earthquake risk and preparedness.[[27]](#cite_note-Hall_2011-27)

In locations where a historical record exists it may be used to estimate the timing, location and magnitude of future seismic events. There are several interpretative factors to consider. The epicentres or foci and magnitudes of historical earthquakes are subject to interpretation meaning it is possible that 5–6 Mw earthquakes described in the historical record could be larger events occurring elsewhere that were felt moderately in the populated areas that produced written records. Documentation in the historic period may be sparse or incomplete, and not give a full picture of the geographic scope of an earthquake, or the historical record may only have earthquake records spanning a few centuries, a very short time frame in a [seismic cycle](/wiki/Seismic_cycle "Seismic cycle").[[28]](#cite_note-historic-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Engineering seismology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seismology&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Engineering seismology")]

Engineering seismology is the study and application of seismology for engineering purposes.[[30]](#cite_note-30) It generally applied to the branch of seismology that deals with the assessment of the seismic hazard of a site or region for the purposes of earthquake engineering. It is, therefore, a link between [earth science](/wiki/Earth_science "Earth science") and [civil engineering](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering").[[31]](#cite_note-:0-31) There are two principal components of engineering seismology. Firstly, studying earthquake history (e.g. historical[[31]](#cite_note-:0-31) and instrumental catalogs[[32]](#cite_note-32) of seismicity) and [tectonics](/wiki/Tectonics "Tectonics")[[33]](#cite_note-33) to assess the earthquakes that could occur in a region and their characteristics and frequency of occurrence. Secondly, studying strong ground motions generated by earthquakes to assess the expected shaking from future earthquakes with similar characteristics. These strong ground motions could either be observations from [accelerometers](/wiki/Accelerometer "Accelerometer") or seismometers or those simulated by computers using various techniques,[[34]](#cite_note-34) which are then often used to develop ground-motion prediction equations[[35]](#cite_note-35) (or ground-motion models)[[1]](http://www.gmpe.org.uk/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20230609005200/http://www.gmpe.org.uk/) 2023-06-09 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine").
